title: US-Iran Deal Nears Finish Line Amid Public Sparring and NATO's European Drawdown
subhead: A Pakistan-brokered memorandum of understanding appears imminent, according to multiple regional officials, but public statements from Washington and Tehran reveal deep mistrust over frozen assets and nuclear terms. Meanwhile, NATO scrambles to fill gaps as the US slashes forces in Europe, and Ukraine launches a campaign to cut Crimea's land bridge by fall.

sections.heading: US and Iran Close to Agreement, But Trust Gap Persists
sections.body:
Three regional officials told the Associated Press Friday that the United States and Iran are close to signing an agreement to end their war, which began February 28 and has effectively shut down oil shipments through the Strait of Hormuz. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi posted on X that an agreement 'has never been closer,' while US Vice President JD Vance said a deal 'has the potential to remake the region and lead to lasting peace.'

But the public messaging reveals fundamental disagreements. President Trump lashed out at Iranian officials on social media Friday, warning 'They better get their act together, and FAST!' The White House has rejected Iranian state media claims about the terms of a draft memorandum as a 'complete fabrication,' insisting that no Iranian funds will be released upfront. Iran is pushing for immediate access to at least $12 billion held in Qatar as a precondition; the US wants any financial relief tied to verifiable compliance.

According to AP, the tentative deal includes terms for removing and destroying Iran's nuclear material and dismantling Tehran's nuclear program. Iran would also agree not to fund terrorist groups. The agreement is expected to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, phase sanctions relief, and release frozen Iranian assets. Pakistan's army chief, Field Marshal Asim Munir, has brokered the talks with backing from Saudi Arabia, Turkey, Egypt, and Qatar.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the most concrete progress toward ending the Iran war in over three months, but the public clash over terms shows how fragile the negotiations remain. The core dispute — whether Iran gets upfront asset relief or must first demonstrate compliance — goes to the heart of the trust deficit between Washington and Tehran. Both sides are signaling to domestic and international audiences that they are not conceding too much.

sections.heading: US Sanctions Iran's Largest Crypto Exchange as Financial Pressure Mounts
sections.body:
On June 2, the US Treasury sanctioned Nobitex, Iran's largest digital asset exchange, for alleged sanctions evasion and terror financing. According to Crypto Briefing, the move targets a critical channel for Iranian financial operations at a moment when Tehran is seeking access to frozen assets worth between $12 billion and $24 billion.

The sanctions create compliance ripple effects. Other exchanges with exposure to Iranian users are likely to accelerate de-risking efforts, potentially resulting in forced liquidations and account freezes across the broader Middle East and Central Asian regions. The timing — during active negotiations — signals that Washington is prepared to keep tightening financial pressure even as talks progress.
sections.whyItMatters: The Nobitex sanctions demonstrate that financial warfare continues alongside diplomatic outreach. For Iran, the sanctions highlight the vulnerability of its alternative financial channels. For the US, they are a reminder that leverage can be applied even while negotiations proceed. The broader pool of frozen assets, held primarily overseas, remains central to any deal.

sections.heading: NATO Scrambles for Plan B as US Cuts Forces in Europe
sections.body:
NATO's top military officer, US General Alex Grynkewich, is weighing alternative plans to defend Europe after the Pentagon warned allies that the US is scaling down its military commitment to focus on Indo-Pacific threats. According to AP, the US plans to withdraw an aircraft carrier and its support group, a submarine, aerial refueling planes, and dozens of fighter jets from the European theater.

The so-called NATO Force Model — the alliance's plan for making forces available in crisis — depends heavily on US assets that are now being pulled. Grynkewich said European allies and Canada should fill the gaps 'now and in the near term' with manned and unmanned aircraft and naval vessels. Washington wants answers by the time President Trump meets his NATO counterparts at a summit in Turkey on July 7-8.

Intelligence reports and Russian troop movements suggest 'Russia is not looking for a conflict with NATO,' Grynkewich said, noting that Moscow is bogged down in Ukraine and struggling to recruit troops. But European governments warn that Russia could be in a position to launch attacks elsewhere on the continent within three to five years if it prevails in Ukraine.
sections.whyItMatters: This represents a structural shift in NATO's posture. For decades, the alliance has assumed American dominance in air and naval power in Europe. The drawdown forces European nations to confront the possibility that they may need to plan for territorial defense without full US air cover. The timing — during the Iran war and Ukraine conflict — amplifies the pressure on European capitals already stretched thin.

sections.heading: UK Defense Secretary Resigns Over Military Spending Dispute
sections.body:
British Defense Secretary John Healey resigned Thursday, saying the government's Defense Investment Plan falls 'well short of what is required at this dangerous time.' According to AP, Healey told Prime Minister Keir Starmer that the Treasury's spending plan would raise defense spending to just 2.68% of GDP by 2030 — far below the 3% by 2035 target that Starmer has pledged.

Healey cited the Iran war, Russia's invasion of Ukraine, and threats from Moscow as reasons Britain cannot afford inadequate defense spending. 'You have been unable, and the Treasury has been unwilling, to commit the resources that the nation needs,' he wrote in his resignation letter. A junior defense minister, Al Carns, also quit. Starmer appointed Dan Jarvis, the former security minister, to replace Healey.

The resignation is another blow to Starmer, who is already facing calls from within Labour to step down. Gen. Richard Barrons, who helped lead a defense review underpinning the investment plan, said the government is 'actively going backwards' by refusing to fund its own review. 'Allies and adversaries alike will be paying attention,' he told AP.
sections.whyItMatters: Healey's resignation underscores the tension between fiscal constraints and security imperatives across NATO. Britain has been a leading voice on Ukraine support and Hormuz maritime security, but the political will to fund that role is running into Treasury resistance. The message from Healey — that Britain cannot defend its commitments at current spending levels — is a warning to the entire alliance.

sections.heading: Ukraine's Fall Campaign: Cutting Crimea's Land Bridge by Drone Blockade
sections.body:
Ukraine is preparing a military-diplomatic campaign to force Putin to negotiate by fall 2026, according to Meduza and Ukrainska Pravda. The strategy combines escalation of long-range drone strikes, denial of Starlink satellite communications to Russian forces, and a breakthrough in medium-range drones that has already turned the Rostov-Simferopol highway into a 'road of death' and triggered a fuel crisis in Crimea.

Ukrainian military sources say the goal is an 'operational drone blockade' that would sever the land corridor to the annexed peninsula. 'Wait until July and you will see what Ukrainian strength is,' one source told Ukrainska Pravda. Ukraine's presidential office sees a potential 'window of opportunity for talks' in October-November — before US elections and after Russian Duma elections.

The campaign follows Zelenskyy's open letter to Putin calling for a face-to-face meeting, which Putin declined. According to sources, Zelenskyy also sent a message through Roman Abramovich, warning that Russian commanders are misleading Putin about the true situation at the front and will not capture all of Donbas by late summer or fall.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine is shifting from a defensive posture to an active pressure campaign designed to create battlefield conditions that make negotiations inevitable. The focus on Crimea — the symbolic and logistical heart of Russian occupation — signals Kyiv's intent to raise the costs of continued war. But the timeline depends on sustained Western support and continued drone warfare innovation.

sections.heading: Gazans Organize Against Hamas as Humanitarian Crisis Deepens
sections.body:
According to the Telegraph, surviving residents of Gaza are being encouraged to take to the streets on June 26 in an online campaign declaring 'You will not break our spirits.' The nascent movement — described as a potential 'revolution' against Hamas — reflects growing desperation and anger after months of war and humanitarian catastrophe.

The campaign emerges amid a broader regional realignment. The US-Iran negotiations are expected to address the Lebanese front, where Hezbollah continues to fight Israel. Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz said Friday that Israel expects Trump to advocate for Israel's interests and that Israel will not withdraw from zones it occupies in Lebanon, Syria, and Gaza, nor from refugee camps in the northern West Bank.
sections.whyItMatters: Internal Palestinian opposition to Hamas would represent a significant shift in Gaza's political landscape. But organizing under current conditions is extremely difficult, and it is unclear whether the June 26 campaign will materialize at scale. For now, it signals that war fatigue is affecting not just external actors but the populations directly under militant control.

bottomLine: A Pakistan-brokered deal to end the US-Iran war appears closer than ever, with both sides signaling progress while publicly clashing over terms. The US insists on compliance before financial relief; Iran wants upfront access to frozen assets. NATO, meanwhile, faces a structural challenge as the US draws down forces in Europe to focus on the Indo-Pacific, forcing allies to fill air and naval gaps they may not be able to afford. Britain's defense secretary resigned over that very gap between commitments and spending. Ukraine is betting on a fall campaign to force negotiations through drone warfare targeting Crimea's land bridge. And inside Gaza, the seeds of resistance to Hamas are emerging — though whether they can take root remains uncertain. The common thread: all these fronts are converging on timelines measured in months, not years. The window for each actor is narrowing.
